\noindent
This supplement collects the complete tables and the one derivation that support
the main text. Section, equation, and table numbers carry an ``S'' prefix;
references of the form ``main-text Section~X,'' ``Eq.~(main~X)'' point to the
primary manuscript. Every table is generated by a committed script under
\texttt{code/round63/figs/} (named in each caption/comment) that reads the frozen
campaign artifact and prints the tabulated numbers; no value is imputed, and
empty or non-numeric cells are dropped from every statistic rather than filled.

% ======================================================================
\section{Why a full heteroscedastic Gaussian likelihood fails at high load}
\label{sec:s1}

The reconstruction of the main text (Eq.~(main~2)) uses the renewal
quasi-likelihood (RQL) data term rather than a full heteroscedastic Gaussian
likelihood in the integrated count. This section shows why the Gaussian model is
not merely less accurate but is \emph{non-coercive} in the high-load regime,
which is the rigorous form of the brightness-inflation pathology that RQL removes
by construction.

Consider modelling the single-frame count $N_i$ of a non-paralyzable detector
(dead time $\tau$, window $T=\nu\tau$, load $\rho_i=\lambda_i\tau$) as a Gaussian
with load-dependent mean and variance, $N_i\sim\mathcal{N}\!\big(\mu(\rho_i),
\sigma^2(\rho_i)\big)$. The per-frame negative log-likelihood is
\begin{equation}
  \ell_i(\lambda_i) \;=\; \tfrac12\log\sigma^2(\rho_i)
  \;+\; \frac{\big(N_i-\mu(\rho_i)\big)^2}{2\,\sigma^2(\rho_i)}
  \;+\; \mathrm{const},
  \label{eq:gaussnll}
\end{equation}
in which the first (``log-det'') term is the heteroscedastic normalization. For
the active-start renewal count, each registered event costs a deterministic dead
time $\tau$ followed by an exponential wait $\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$, so the
inter-event time has mean $(1+\rho)/\lambda$ and variance $1/\lambda^2$. Renewal
theory then gives, to leading order in $\nu$,
\begin{equation}
  \mu(\rho) \;=\; \nu\,\frac{\rho}{1+\rho},
  \qquad
  \sigma^2(\rho) \;=\; \nu\,\frac{\rho}{(1+\rho)^3}.
  \label{eq:meanvar}
\end{equation}
The mean is monotone in $\rho$, but the variance is not: differentiating the
log-det term,
\begin{equation}
  \frac{\partial}{\partial\rho}\,\tfrac12\log\sigma^2(\rho)
  \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\rho}-\frac{3}{1+\rho}\right)
  \;=\; \frac{1-2\rho}{2\,\rho(1+\rho)}.
  \label{eq:logdetderiv}
\end{equation}
The sign of this ``log-det incentive'' \emph{reverses at} $\rho=\tfrac12$: the
modelled count variance rises with load for $\rho<\tfrac12$ and falls for
$\rho>\tfrac12$, peaking at the mid-load point where roughly half of each slot is
occupied by dead time. Below $\rho=\tfrac12$ the normalization penalizes an
inflated fitted rate; \emph{above} $\rho=\tfrac12$ it rewards one, because a
larger $\lambda$ shrinks $\sigma^2$ and drives $\tfrac12\log\sigma^2\to-\infty$.

This is decisive at ceiling counts. When a frame saturates, $N_i\tau\ge T$
(equivalently $N_i\ge\nu$), the count can no longer discriminate among large
rates, and the plug-in variance $\sigma^2(\rho)\to0$ as $\rho\to\infty$ while the
squared-residual term stays bounded. Objective~\eqref{eq:gaussnll} is then
\emph{unbounded below} in $\lambda_i$: the Gaussian estimator can lower its loss
without limit by inflating the brightness of any saturated measurement, so the
maximum-likelihood/MAP problem is non-coercive and its minimizer is ill-posed.
The exhibited heteroscedastic-Gaussian pseudo-likelihood is noncoercive along
the displayed high-load direction, and the tested TV settings did not remove
the resulting failure. Sufficiently coercive penalties can bound such a
direction, so no universal claim is made over all convex regularizers.

RQL removes the pathology at its source. Its data term (main-text Eq.~(main~2)),
$\frac{1}{M}\sum_i\big[(T-N_i\tau)\lambda_i(x)-N_i\log\lambda_i(x)\big]$, retains
only the renewal \emph{mean} relationship---its single-measurement stationary
point $\hat\lambda=N/(T-N\tau)$ is exactly the classical dead-time rate
correction---and carries no $\tfrac12\log\sigma^2$ term. It is convex and coercive
in $\lambda$ (and, through the affine map, in $x\ge0$); the only genuine
identifiability boundary that survives is the exact-count ceiling $N\tau\ge T$,
at which the objective is correctly reported as unbounded and the ceiling
fraction is recorded per cell rather than silently fitted. The convex surrogate
thus keeps the correct mean response while discarding the variance-collapse term
that makes the full Gaussian likelihood fail deep in the dead-time regime; that
the surrogate costs essentially nothing in reconstruction quality is verified
against the exact likelihood in Section~\ref{sec:s4}.

\section{Full NATURAL-30 cohort}
\label{sec:s2}

Table~\ref{tab:s2_natural} lists the complete per-image acquisition-speed
endpoint for the preregistered natural-image cohort (secondary, no gate; the
same dense-pattern protocol as Study~1), from which the main text reports only
the cohort summary and one image pair. The cohort spans two synthetic stress
scenes: \texttt{low\_contrast} is the only strongly negative rung
($\Delta Q=-1.78$~dB) and \texttt{fine\_lines} sits essentially at zero
($\Delta Q=+0.07$~dB), bracketing the smooth-scene low-benefit regime.

\input{supp_tables/S2_natural.tex}

% ======================================================================
\section{Complete detector-mismatch tables}
\label{sec:s3}

The main text reports six anchor perturbations at the preregistered operating
point; the anchor subset is reproduced in Table~\ref{tab:s3_anchors} and the
complete variant sweep in Table~\ref{tab:s3_full}. The full table adds the
severity sweeps (dead-time error to $\pm20\%$, dark rate $5$--$50\%$,
afterpulsing to $10\%$) and the start-mode, guard-time and dead-time-jitter axes.
Three honesty notes are carried explicitly: (i)~at the safe point PRECORRECT is a
degenerate floored reconstruction ($\mathrm{flux\_dev}=-1.0$, identical to its
own baseline), so its safe dPSNR is uninformative and is omitted from the anchor
columns; (ii)~the dark50\%-known PRECORRECT safe row is an \emph{un-flooring
anomaly}---a known $50\%$ dark rate lifts PRECORRECT off its floor
($\mathrm{flux\_dev}\!\to\!0$) and yields a $-2.60$~dB paired shift, the single
large PRECORRECT swing in the table; and (iii)~the paralyzable-data /
non-paralyzable-model anchor is not instantiated in the pilot and is reported
\textsc{missing} rather than imputed.

\input{supp_tables/S3_anchors.tex}
\input{supp_tables/S3_full.tex}

% ======================================================================
\section{Complete exact-likelihood-versus-RQL comparison}
\label{sec:s4}

Because RQL is a convex surrogate for the exact renewal likelihood, the main text
verifies at $8^2$ and $16^2$ that it tracks the exact likelihood under an
identical TV functional and the identical frozen selection rule.
Table~\ref{tab:s4_grid} gives the complete $12\times4$ grid of the median
exact-minus-RQL radiometric-PSNR gap and the pair-level statistics over all $864$
paired reconstructions. The median gap is negligible throughout the deployment
zone and grows only into the extreme-load corner; individual cells differ in both
directions (up to $9.23$~dB), consistent with the nonconvexity of the exact
objective and with no systematic advantage to either arm.

\input{supp_tables/S4_grid.tex}

% ======================================================================
\section{No-gate occupancy-ladder tables}
\label{sec:s5}

Table~\ref{tab:s5_ladder} gives the per-image fixed-dwell high-flux gain on every
rung of the frozen occupancy ladder (per-image seed-mean radiometric PSNR at
$\rhobar=0.6$ minus $\rhobar=0.05$, both at $\nu=2000$, under equal mean detector
load and equal incident dose across occupancies). The gate-carrying $k=16$ column
is the frozen $\Delta Q_{\mathrm{budget}}$ from the Study-2 analyzer; the
$k=512/32/1$ columns are the no-gate control and robustness rungs. The cohort
medians ($+0.64$, $+4.13$, $+1.16$, $+3.56$~dB at $k=512,32,16,1$) are nonmonotone
in sparsity---$k=32$ attains the largest median gain---exposing the
contrast--conditioning trade-off discussed in the main text.

\input{supp_tables/S5_ladder.tex}

% ======================================================================
\section{Dynamic-scattering detail}
\label{sec:s6}

Table~\ref{tab:s6_scattering} details the dynamic-scattering secondary
(multiplicative lognormal AR(1) factors, $\mathrm{CV}(\alpha)=0.20$, applied
before dead time; $k=16$ geometry, six frozen mechanism representatives), giving
the mean and sample sd of the RQL radiometric PSNR at each $(\rhobar,\nu)$ and the
per-$\nu$ high-flux gain. The gain persists and grows with dwell ($+0.66$,
$+1.84$, $+2.32$~dB at $\nu=20,200,2000$).

\input{supp_tables/S6_scattering.tex}

% ======================================================================
\section{Ensemble and undersampling sensitivity (S2B/S2C)}
\label{sec:s7}

Tables~\ref{tab:s7_s2b} and~\ref{tab:s7_s2c} report the supplementary
geometry/ensemble sensitivity: the undersampling budget $M\in\{1024,2048\}$ for
dense Bernoulli$(1/2)$ illumination (S2B), and the complementary Hadamard-type
pairs (\texttt{hadpair}) and gamma-modulated patterns (\texttt{gam4}) at $M=2048$
(S2C). Entries are median RQL radiometric PSNR at $\rhobar\in\{0.05,0.6,1.0\}$ and
$\nu\in\{100,500,2000\}$. Consistent with the multiplex-limited reading of the
dense regime, the RQL quality is stable across sampling budgets and pattern
ensembles and rises modestly with load at long dwell. (Provenance note: the S2C
pilot table is a regenerated supplementary artifact; its fragment records its
provenance separately from the frozen campaign outputs. The R19 correction
route applies only to the paper-2 elapsed-time endpoint.)

\input{supp_tables/S7_ensemble.tex}

% ======================================================================
\section{Descriptive measurement-audit distributions}
\label{sec:s8}

Table~\ref{tab:s8_audit} summarizes the descriptive measurement audit on the RQL
arm of the $64^2$ detail cohort: the refit-bootstrap discrepancy-ratio
$d_{\mathrm{ratio}}$ quantiles and the leak-suspect counts by $(\rhobar,\nu)$,
with the overall audit-status tally in the caption. Two caveats from the main
text are repeated here. The audit is descriptive and is \emph{not} a
model-adequacy test: every audit statistic is excluded from regularization
selection, reconstruction, cell inclusion, and all confirmatory pass/fail
decisions. Its refit-bootstrap reference distribution is conditional on a
smoothed plug-in scene, so the empirical tail ranks are not calibrated
model-class $p$-values and can be anti-conservative in the upper tail when the
true scene is rougher than the plug-in---the widening upper $d_{\mathrm{ratio}}$
quantiles at long dwell and high load reflect this plug-in conditioning, not a
detected model failure.

\input{supp_tables/S8_audit.tex}

\end{document}
